refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total operational time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement for remote equipment control. Refinement clarifies the measurement basis (bidirectional link), acceptance threshold (0.999), and evaluation window. High safety criticality demands explicit availability targets to prevent loss-of-control scenarios.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during field trials; calculation of availability as (total_uptime / total_evaluation_period); comparison against 0.999 threshold with statistical confidence interval.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and alert the Safety Manager within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or failover mechanisms to achieve ≥ 0.999 availability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single wireless link with no redundancy; rely on network provider SLA.
DP-B: Dual independent wireless links (primary + backup) with automatic failover; active monitoring and health checks.
DP-C: Hybrid wired + wireless with automatic switchover; continuous link quality assessment.
DP-D: Mesh network topology with multi-hop routing and local relay stations; distributed link management.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent wireless links (primary + backup) with automatic failover; active monitoring and health checks.

potential_sources: Failover logic couples command and telemetry paths; must ensure atomic switchover to prevent command-telemetry desynchronization.
Link health monitoring couples with Safety Services for alert generation; interface contract must be explicit.
Dual link provisioning couples with Vehicle power budget and antenna placement; physical constraints must be validated.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is decoupled from specific wireless technology (4G/5G/proprietary), allowing flexibility. Coupling is manageable via explicit failover state machine and interface contracts. Recommended for high-criticality application.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent wireless carriers or frequency bands are available at the worksite.
Assumption: Vehicle has sufficient power budget and antenna space for dual transceivers.
Risk: Correlated failure mode (e.g., both links affected by RF interference) could violate availability target; mitigation requires geographic/frequency diversity analysis.
Risk: Failover latency (switchover time) must be < 100 ms to meet safety alert requirement; requires real-time OS and deterministic scheduling.
Risk: Link health check frequency and timeout thresholds must be tuned to avoid false positives (unnecessary failovers) while detecting real failures quickly.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to specify: (1) failover latency budget (< 100 ms), (2) link health check interval (e.g., 50 ms heartbeat), (3) timeout threshold (e.g., 3 consecutive missed heartbeats = link down), (4) diversity requirement (geographic separation, frequency bands, or carriers).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-link teleoperation subsystem including command reception, telemetry transmission, link health monitoring, failover logic, and interface to Safety Services for alert generation. Includes both vehicle-side (transceiver, link state machine) and tele-operator-side (command encoder, telemetry decoder, link status display) components.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator console; must be delivered with < 100 ms latency and guaranteed ordering.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality metrics) to tele-operator console and monitoring systems; must be continuous and timestamped.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals link degradation, failover events, and loss-of-link conditions to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency procedures.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts the underlying wireless carriers/bands; Teleoperation module requests dual-link provisioning and receives link state notifications (up/down/degraded).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of dual-link failover logic, link health monitoring, and command/telemetry encoding. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary. High cohesion within module.

Vehicle could own the vehicle-side transceiver and link state machine, but command/telemetry logic and failover orchestration would diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation. Not recommended as primary owner.
Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert but not own link availability logic. Ownership here would create tight coupling and diffuse teleoperation concerns.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; assigning IM here would bypass architectural layering and prevent clear ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns the complete dual-link failover subsystem, including link health monitoring, failover state machine, and SafetyAlert generation. Vehicle module owns only the vehicle-side transceiver hardware and link state reporting. This separation maintains cohesion and minimizes diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module is not clearly scoped, transceiver firmware updates and link state reporting could diffuse across module boundaries.
Risk: Failover latency depends on both Teleoperation logic and Vehicle-side link state detection; timing contract must be explicit.
Risk: Network Link interface abstraction must hide carrier-specific details; if not, Teleoperation logic could become tightly coupled to specific wireless technologies.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define explicit interface contracts: (1) Network Link provides link state (up/down/degraded) with < 50 ms latency, (2) Teleoperation failover logic guarantees < 100 ms switchover, (3) SafetyAlert is generated within 100 ms of link loss detection, (4) Vehicle module owns only transceiver hardware and reports link state; Teleoperation owns failover orchestration.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual independent links with failover) directly addresses FR requirement for ≥ 0.999 availability. Dual redundancy is a proven approach for high-availability systems. Assumptions (carrier availability, power budget) are reasonable for NGHE context. Coupling is manageable via explicit state machine and interface contracts.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic must not diffuse across Teleoperation and Vehicle boundaries; clear ownership of link state machine is critical.
SafetyAlert generation couples Teleoperation to Safety Services; interface contract must be explicit to prevent tight coupling.
Network Link abstraction must be enforced; if Teleoperation directly accesses carrier-specific APIs, diffusion risk increases.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. Recommended ownership is clear and justified. Diffusion risks are manageable via explicit interface contracts and module boundary enforcement.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability measurement is feasible via continuous link state monitoring and uptime calculation. Field trials can validate against 0.999 threshold. Failover latency can be measured via instrumentation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module with clear sub-FRs for command link, telemetry link, alert generation, and redundancy. Verification criteria are measurable.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command link, telemetry link, alert generation, redundancy). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. DP-B is sound and well-justified, but implementation details (failover latency, link health check parameters, diversity strategy) must be elaborated at the next level. Teleoperation module assignment is appropriate and well-contained.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle with < 100 ms latency.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator with < 100 ms latency.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and alert the Safety Manager within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall implement dual-link failover with < 100 ms switchover latency and link health checks at 50 ms intervals.

assumptions: Two independent wireless carriers or frequency bands are available at the worksite.
Vehicle has sufficient power budget and antenna space for dual transceivers.
Network Link interface provides link state notifications with < 50 ms latency.
Safety Services is available to consume SafetyAlert signals.
Evaluation period is at least 30 days of continuous operation to achieve statistical confidence in 0.999 availability measurement.
open_risks: Correlated failure mode (both links affected by RF interference) could violate availability target; mitigation requires geographic/frequency diversity analysis.
Failover latency must be validated in field trials; if > 100 ms, DP-B may not be sufficient.
Link health check tuning (interval, timeout threshold) requires empirical validation to avoid false positives and false negatives.
SafetyAlert interface contract must be explicit; if Safety Services cannot consume alerts within required latency, system-level redesign may be needed.
evidence_needed: Wireless carrier availability and SLA documentation for worksite.
Vehicle power budget and antenna placement analysis.
Network Link interface specification with latency and reliability guarantees.
Safety Services alert consumption specification and latency requirements.
Field trial data on link availability, failover latency, and false positive rates.
Diversity analysis (geographic separation, frequency bands, carriers) to mitigate correlated failures.
